metadata.title: B1 Preliminary Gold Mock 2
metadata.description: M4.1 academic reference mock for B1 Preliminary — Media, Environment, and Lifestyle themes across reading, writing, listening, and speaking.
metadata.authoringNotes: M4.1 Gold Mock — manually authored and academically reviewed.

parts.title: Part 1 — Listen and choose the correct picture
parts.instructions: Part 1 — Listen and choose the correct picture

audio.transcript: Girl: I'm recording a short video review in the studio. Boy: At the beach, we're carrying rubbish bags and gloves for the clean-up.
audio.caption: Part 1 — Listen and choose the correct picture

parts.title: Part 2 — Listen and choose the best answer
parts.instructions: Part 2 — Listen and choose the best answer

audio.transcript: Boy: I missed film club because I had to finish editing. Girl: Next weekend I'm joining a river clean-up with friends.
audio.caption: Part 2 — Listen and choose the best answer

parts.title: Part 3 — Listen and complete each gap
parts.instructions: Part 3 — Listen and complete each gap

audio.transcript: Organiser: The eco-film festival starts in April. Student tickets cost eight pounds. Bring a reusable bottle.
audio.caption: Part 3 — Listen and complete each gap

audio.transcript: Speaker: I learned to edit at a community media centre. I limit streaming before bed and compost kitchen waste at home.
audio.caption: Part 4 — Listen to the monologue

parts.title: Part 5 — Listen and match each speaker to an activity
parts.instructions: Part 5 — Listen and match each speaker to an activity

audio.transcript: Five students describe activities: school radio, vegan cooking club, tree planting, photography society, and recycling volunteering.
audio.caption: Part 5 — Listen and match each speaker to an activity

questions.questionText: No filming without permission — media team badge required at all events.

choices.text: Only staff with a badge may record video.
The media team has finished work.

questions.questionText: Bike lane closed for tree planting — cyclists use Riverside Path until Friday.

choices.text: Cyclists should take an alternative route this week.
The bike lane is permanently removed.

questions.questionText: Farmers' market — organic produce and reusable bag discount every Saturday.

choices.text: Shoppers save money if they bring their own bags.
The market sells only imported goods.
Organic food is unavailable on Saturdays.

questions.questionText: Podcast recording in progress — please speak quietly in the corridor.

choices.text: People nearby should keep noise down.
The corridor is closed all day.

questions.questionText: Wellness week — free yoga at lunchtime; register online by Wednesday.

choices.text: Students must sign up on the website before Wednesday.

questions.questionText: Solar panel tour — learn how the school reduces its carbon footprint.

choices.text: Visitors can see how clean energy is used at school.
Solar panels are not installed yet.
The tour is only for teachers.

questions.questionText: Who hosts a weekly podcast about music?
Who leads a beach clean-up club?
Who writes reviews for a lifestyle blog?
Who edits videos for an environmental channel?
Who is planning a car-free day campaign?

pairs.rightText: is going to ask the council for support

questions.questionText: Why did Leo start his podcast?

content.passage: When Leo started a school podcast about local music, he never expected hundreds of listeners. Each week he interviews bands and reviews new albums, editing episodes in a free studio the library offers. Teachers noticed his confidence growing in presentations, and classmates began sharing story ideas for future episodes. After a feature on eco-friendly festivals, a community radio station invited Leo to co-host a live show about sustainable events. The experience taught him that creative media projects can connect people while promoting causes he cares about.

choices.text: To share interviews and reviews about local music
To work for a commercial radio station

questions.questionText: How did teachers react to Leo's work?

content.passage: When Leo started a school podcast about local music, he never expected hundreds of listeners. Each week he interviews bands and reviews new albums, editing episodes in a free studio the library offers. Teachers noticed his confidence growing in presentations, and classmates began sharing story ideas for future episodes. After a feature on eco-friendly festivals, a community radio station invited Leo to co-host a live show about sustainable events. The experience taught him that creative media projects can connect people while promoting causes he cares about.

choices.text: They noticed his confidence in presentations
They asked him to stop interviewing bands

questions.questionText: Gap (1): become more ___ about going online

content.passage: Streaming services have changed how teenagers discover films and series. Instead of waiting for weekly broadcasts, viewers can watch entire seasons in one weekend. Critics warn that binge-watching may reduce sleep, yet many students say it helps them relax after exams. Documentaries about climate change are especially popular because they combine entertainment with useful facts. Some schools now discuss media habits in class, encouraging pupils to balance screen time with outdoor activities. Parents who set clear limits often find that children become more (1) ___ about when they go online. Teachers recommend keeping devices out of bedrooms so evenings remain (2) ___ for rest. When families watch a film together, they can talk about messages on recycling or healthy lifestyles, making screen time more (3) ___. Community cinemas still matter because they offer shared experiences that streaming cannot (4) ___. Local festivals also promote films made by young directors, giving students a chance to be (5) ___ in real events. In the long run, thoughtful media choices support both mental health and environmental (6) ___ awareness.

questions.questionText: Which sentence introduces the problem of ocean pollution?

content.passage: [A] Plastic waste in oceans harms marine life and enters the food chain. [B] Many coastal towns now organise monthly beach clean-ups led by volunteers. [C] Some teenagers film short documentaries about what they collect and share them online. [D] These videos encourage viewers to use reusable bottles and bags. [E] Local shops have responded by offering discounts to customers who bring their own containers. [F] Small lifestyle changes, when shared through media, can inspire wider community action.

choices.text: [A] Plastic waste in oceans harms marine life and enters the food chain.
[C] Some teenagers film short documentaries about what they collect and share them online.
[E] Local shops have responded by offering discounts to customers who bring their own containers.

questions.questionText: Write an email in response to your English teacher about media.

content.prompt: Your English teacher has asked you to write about how young people use social media responsibly. Write an email to your teacher.
content.taskDescription: Write about 100 words. Answer all three points.
requiredPoints: Describe one social media platform you use and why
Explain how you balance screen time with other activities
Suggest one school project that could promote healthy media habits

questions.questionText: Write a story about environment that begins with the sentence below.

content.prompt: Your teacher has asked you to write a story. Your story must begin with this sentence: 'The beach was cleaner than I had ever seen it, thanks to our volunteer team.'

questions.questionText: What is the girl doing in the photo studio?
What are the students carrying on the beach?
Where is the man buying a reusable cup?
What are the friends watching on the tablet?
What activity are the cyclists promoting?
Why did the boy miss the film club meeting?

choices.text: He had to finish an editing project

questions.questionText: What is the woman going to do next weekend?
How often does the wellness podcast release episodes?
What must visitors do before entering the media lab?
What has the family recently started doing?

choices.text: Meal planning to reduce food waste

questions.questionText: Where did the speaker first learn to edit videos?
Why does the speaker limit streaming before bed?
What change has the speaker made at home?
How often does the speaker upload podcast episodes?
What advice does the speaker give to new volunteers?

choices.text: Start with a small task and share your progress online
Work alone until you feel confident

pairs.rightText: sorting materials at a recycling hub

questions.questionText: Answer the examiner's questions about media.

content.prompt: The examiner will ask about how you use media, your hobbies, and how you spend your free time.
followUpQuestions: What kind of films or series do you enjoy watching?
Do you prefer listening to music or podcasts? Why?
How much time do you spend online on a typical school day?
Have you ever created content for social media or a school project?
What would you like to learn about media production?

questions.questionText: Describe the lifestyle and say what you think is happening.

content.prompt: Look at the photograph. Describe the people, the place, and explain why they might be there.

questions.questionText: Discuss ways young people can live more sustainably.

content.prompt: Talk with your partner about lifestyle changes that protect the environment in your town.
followUpQuestions: Which change would be easiest for students to start?
How could social media help spread environmental messages?
What problems might people face when changing habits?
Should schools ban single-use plastic completely?
How could local businesses support greener lifestyles?
Which idea would make the biggest difference long term?

coverageAchieved.notes: Authored for M3.1 Gold Mock specification.
